food_name: Crisp Grape Boom (Grape Jasmine Green Tea Drink)
food_subgroup: Fruit-infused tea drinks with real fruit

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for grape-jasmine green tea fruit drinks specifically; closest parent 'tea beverage' (FOODON_03301099) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 AN0AN covers tea and tea-based beverages; no specific code for fruit-infused jasmine green tea with fresh grape exists. Closest parent applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 34111008 'Green tea (substance)' is the closest defensible anchor; the full drink with grape and jasmine is not individually coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard HeyTea cup of Crisp Grape Boom is approximately 400 ml, containing brewed jasmine green tea, fresh whole or halved grapes, and cane sugar syrup, served chilled over ice.

nutrient_source.title: Grapes, raw (USDA FoodData Central ID 174683) and Green tea, brewed (USDA FoodData Central ID 173228); composite estimation for grape-jasmine green tea beverage
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry exists for HeyTea Crisp Grape Boom in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by composite modelling: approximately 30% fresh grapes by weight (USDA FoodData Central ID 174683, raw grapes) and 70% brewed jasmine green tea (USDA FoodData Central ID 173228, green tea brewed). Sugar contribution from cane sugar syrup is estimated at approximately 8 g per 100 ml based on standard HeyTea sweetness level (100% sugar = approximately 20 g per 400 ml serving, yielding ~5 g/100 ml; mid-range estimate used at 8 g/100 g to account for grape natural sugars plus added syrup). Energy calculated from macros using Atwater factors. All values are estimates and should be verified against brand nutritional disclosure when available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for grapes and sweetened tea beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for HeyTea Crisp Grape Boom. GI estimated as analogous to sweetened grape juice / fresh grape beverages. University of Sydney GI Database lists grapes at GI 46–59 (mean ~53, glucose reference). Sweetened green tea beverages with fruit are estimated in the low-to-medium range. Composite GI of 52 applied (low category, glucose reference = 100) based on weighted contribution of grape sugars (fructose-dominant, GI ~46–59) and added cane sugar (sucrose, GI ~65), diluted by tea base. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (52 × 40.8 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 21.2, rounded to 20.4 using 39.2 g net carbohydrate (dietary fibre subtracted). Documented as analogous estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~3 mg/100 g); potassium present from grapes
hypertension.guidance: Crisp Grape Boom has negligible sodium (~12 mg per 400 ml serving) and provides modest potassium from fresh grapes, making it a low hypertension concern beverage; however, individuals should request reduced or no added sugar to avoid excess caloric intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added cane sugar and natural grape sugars contribute approximately 38 g of sugar per standard 400 ml serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request 'no sugar added' or minimum sugar level when ordering Crisp Grape Boom, as the default preparation contains approximately 38 g of total sugars per serving (GI ~52, GL ~20), which can cause a meaningful postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; antioxidant polyphenols from green tea and grapes
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crisp Grape Boom is very low in saturated fat and cholesterol, and the jasmine green tea and grape components provide catechins and resveratrol-related polyphenols with potential cardioprotective properties; the primary cardiovascular concern is the added sugar content, which should be minimised for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crisp Grape Boom is a signature fruit-infused tea drink from HeyTea (喜茶), a premium Chinese tea beverage chain with outlets in Singapore including Marina Bay Sands. The drink combines selected fresh grapes — typically green or red seedless varieties — with a brewed jasmine-scented green tea base, served chilled over ice and sweetened with cane sugar syrup by default. The flavour profile is refreshing and lightly floral, with the natural sweetness and slight tartness of fresh grapes complementing the delicate jasmine aroma of the green tea. The texture includes the juicy burst of whole or halved grapes alongside the smooth, lightly astringent tea liquid. It has been a best-seller since 2018. The drink is classified as a minimally processed fruit-tea beverage. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 42 kcal, 10.2 g carbohydrate (predominantly sugars at 9.5 g), 0.3 g protein, and 0.1 g fat. The sodium content is very low (~3 mg/100 g), making it suitable for low-sodium dietary patterns. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low category) based on the grape and sucrose sugar profile, but the glycemic load per standard 400 ml serving is approximately 20, which is clinically meaningful for individuals managing blood glucose. Green tea polyphenols (catechins, EGCG) and grape-derived flavonoids contribute antioxidant activity. The drink is vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-suitable. Sugar-free sweetener options may be available seasonally; customers are advised to request 'no sugar added' or reduced sugar levels to lower the glycemic impact. This record is relevant for dietary counselling contexts involving beverage sugar intake, postprandial glucose management, and antioxidant-rich beverage choices in Southeast Asian urban food environments.
common_user_queries: What is Crisp Grape Boom from HeyTea?

confidence_reason: No direct nutritional entry for HeyTea Crisp Grape Boom exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). All nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for raw grapes and brewed green tea, with added sugar estimated from standard HeyTea sweetness conventions. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database grape and sucrose data. Confidence is low pending brand-level nutritional disclosure or laboratory analysis.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no direct approved-source entry exists for this branded drink. Vitamin A, B-vitamins (B1, B2, B3, B6, B12), folate, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, and selenium are reported as -1 because these micronutrients are not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries used for estimation and cannot be reliably derived from the composite model without introducing unsupported precision. 2. Saturated fat is 0.0 g per 100 g: confirmed zero by formulation — this is a fruit-tea beverage with no dairy, no oils, and no animal-derived fat-containing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central source components. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are 0.0 g: naturally zero for a dilute fruit-tea beverage with negligible fat content. Trans fat is 0.0 g: naturally zero as no hydrogenated fats are present. Cholesterol is 0.0 g: naturally zero as this is a plant-based beverage with no animal products. 3. GI set to 52 as an analogous estimate (low category) based on University of Sydney GI Database data for grapes and sucrose; glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated at 20.4 g. 4. The description notes that sugar-free sweetener is currently out of stock; the default preparation uses real cane sugar. Customers managing blood glucose should request 'no sugar added'. 5. Ontology codes use closest_parent match type throughout as no exact ontology term exists for this specific branded fruit-tea drink. 6. This food is classified under 'Desserts' in the field survey source but is more accurately a 'Beverages' food group entry; food_group corrected to Beverages per nutritional classification standards. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the food is served in Singapore. 8. All -1 sentinel values represent micronutrients not reported in the USDA FoodData Central source components used for this composite estimate.
